1.description: Hazard-of-sale ratio for fund managers’ selling decisions. This figure presents estimated hazard ratios comparing Ancerno fund managers’ hazard-of-sale at various levels of return since purchase with their baseline hazard-of-sale for stocks with unrealized returns close to 0 (−2% to 2%). Hazard ratios are estimated from Cox proportional hazard regressions of model (2) in the text. The black line plots estimated hazard ratios based on the mean coefficient estimates from 100 randomly drawn samples, each containing the investor-stock-days from 10,000 round-trip stock positions taken by Ancerno fund managers. The resulting randomly drawn samples contain an average of 1,161,766 investor-stock-days per estimation sample. These random samples are not conditioned on their proximity to earnings announcement dates (i.e., they estimate the unconditional disposition effect for Ancerno fund managers). Dashed lines indicate 95% confidence intervals for estimated hazard ratios based on the mean standard errors from the 100 estimation samples. A formal definition for return since purchase (RET) appears in the Appendix. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Hazard-of-sale ratios for fund managers’ announcement-window selling decisions. This figure presents estimated conditional hazard ratios comparing Ancerno fund managers’ hazard-of-sale at various levels of return since purchase with their baseline hazard-of-sale for stocks with unrealized returns close to 0 (−2% to 2%) and with median levels of quarterly earnings news. The estimation sample includes 1,113,802 investor-stock-days observed during the (−1,+1) trading day windowed centered around quarterly earnings announcements, as described in TableI. Panel A plots the estimated hazard ratio for each 4%-wide return category included in model (3), estimated at the median level of quarterly earnings news and at the medians of all other control variables included in the model. Panel B plots the estimated hazard ratio associated with a one-unit change I couldn't select the words I want in this paragraph because of the “uncorrected proof”. Anyways, model (1c) should say model (3) now in unexpected earnings for each 4%-wide return category, estimated at the median level of all other control variables included in model. A one-unit increase in unexpected earnings is equivalent to an increase from the lowest to highest earnings news deciles. Dashed lines indicate 95% confidence intervals for estimated hazard ratios based on robust standard errors. Variable definitions for unexpected earnings (UE) and return since purchase (RET) appear in the Appendix. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Hazard-of-sale ratios for fund manager's announcement-window selling decisions, conditioned on returns since purchase and unexpected earnings news. This figure presents estimated conditional hazard ratios comparing stockholders’ hazard-of-sale at various levels of unexpected earnings and return since purchase with the baseline hazard for stocks with unrealized returns from −2% to 2% at the median level of quarterly earnings news. The estimated hazard ratio is calculated for each decile of unexpected earnings within each 4%-wide return category included in model (3). These estimated hazard ratios are presented as a three-dimensional surface where they-axis (x-axis) denotes values of unexpected earnings (returns since purchase), and estimated hazard ratios are plotted, in color, on thez-axis. Lighter-shaded regions correspond to higher estimated selling probabilities, and darker-shaded regions correspond to lower estimated selling probabilities, relative to the baseline hazard rate. The color bar to the right of each panel denotes the shading scale used to display estimated hazard ratios. Panel A presents a “heat map” of the estimated surface, viewed from above, while Panel B offers a three-dimensional view of the same surface from −15° azimuth, 35° elevation. Variable definitions for unexpected earnings (UE) and return since purchase (RET) appear in the Appendix. All conditional hazard ratios are estimated at the medians of the control variables specified in model (3).

4.description: Mean announcement-window abnormal returns for portfolios formed on unexpected earnings and stockholders’ capital gains overhang. This figure depicts mean three-day abnormal returns (BHAR(−1,1)) centered on earnings announcement dates, based on independent sorts of unexpected earnings (UE) and stockholders’ capital gains overhang (CGO). Each calendar quarter, announcements are independently sorted into quintiles ofUEandCGO. Three-day buy-and-hold abnormal returns centered on the earnings announcement date (BHAR(−1,1)) are calculated relative to benchmark returns from the Fama-French-momentum four-factor model (Carhart (1997)). Panel A presents mean abnormal returns byCGOquintile, within each quintile of unexpected earnings (UE). Lighter colored left-hand bars represent lower quintiles ofCGOand darker colored right-hand bars represent higher quintiles ofCGO. Within each quintile ofUE, I test the prediction that mean abnormal returns are decreasing inCGOby regressingBHAR(−1,1)onCGOquintile. *,**, and *** indicate (one-tailed) significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% level, respectively, based on standard errors two-way clustered by firm and event date. Panel B presents mean returns for low and highUEquintiles with the highest (positive) and lowest (negative) spreads in capital gains. Spread quintiles are extremeCGOquintiles with the same sign asUE(i.e., LowUE, LowCGOand HighUE, HighCGO). Negative spread quintiles are extremeCGOquintiles whereCGOis news-contrarian (i.e., LowUE, HighCGOand HighUE, LowCGO). *,**, and *** indicate that differences in abnormal returns are statistically significant at the (two-tailed) 10%, 5%, and 1% level, respectively, based on standard errors two-way clustered by firm and event date. All variables are defined in the Appendix.

5.description: Mean 60-day postannouncement abnormal returns for portfolios formed on unexpected earnings and stockholders’ capital gains overhang. This figure depicts mean 60-day abnormal returns (BHAR(2,61)) following earnings announcements, based on independent sorts of unexpected earnings (UE) and stockholders’ capital gains overhang (CGO). Each calendar quarter, announcements are independently sorted into quintiles ofUEandCGO. Sixty-day buy-and-hold abnormal returns beginning two days after the earnings announcement date (BHAR(2,61)) are calculated relative to benchmark returns from the Fama-French-momentum four-factor model (Carhart (1997)). Panel A presents mean abnormal returns byCGOquintile, within each quintile of unexpected earnings (UE). Lighter colored left-hand bars represent lower quintiles ofCGO, and darker colored right-hand bars represent higher quintiles ofCGO. Within each quintile ofUE, I test the prediction that mean abnormal returns are increasing inCGOby regressingBHAR(2,61)onCGOquintile. *,**, and *** indicate (one-tailed) significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% level, respectively, based on standard errors two-way clustered by firm and event date. Panel B presents mean returns for low and highUEquintiles with the highest (positive) and lowest (negative) spreads in capital gains. Spread quintiles are extremeCGOquintiles with the same sign asUE(i.e., LowUE, LowCGOand HighUE, HighCGO). Negative spread quintiles are extremeCGOquintiles whereCGOis news-contrarian (i.e., LowUE, HighCGOand HighUE, LowCGO). *,**, and *** indicate that differences in abnormal returns are statistically significant at the (two-tailed) 10%, 5%, and 1% level, respectively, based on standard errors two-way clustered by firm and event date. All variables are defined in the Appendix.
